2e36cf35-5278-45ca-a74b-5338811a4026.question: What narratives emerged in the past 7 days, ranked by discussion volume?

plan.objective: Identify and rank major narratives that have emerged in the past 7 days, based on discussion volume.
assumptions: Data is available for the past 7 days.
Discussion volume is measurable (e.g., post/comment counts or user activity).
Narratives can be extracted and clustered from text data.
Ranking is based solely on discussion volume.

steps.title: Collect Discussion Data for Last 7 Days
steps.rationale: Gather all relevant textual discussions (posts, comments) from the last 7 days as input for narrative discovery.
steps.outputSummary: Raw discussion dataset for target time window.

steps.title: Extract and Cluster Narratives
steps.rationale: Identify emergent narratives by clustering and analyzing discussion text.
steps.outputSummary: List of detected narratives with grouped text data.

steps.title: Quantify Discussion Volume per Narrative
steps.rationale: For each narrative cluster, quantify the discussion volume (number of posts/comments, participants, etc.).
steps.outputSummary: Discussion volume metrics for each narrative.

steps.title: Rank Narratives by Discussion Volume
steps.rationale: Sort identified narratives in descending order of their discussion volume to highlight the most significant ones.
steps.outputSummary: Ranked list of narratives by volume.

steps.title: Summarize and Format Results
steps.rationale: Generate concise summaries for each narrative and ensure output fits the required strict typed JSON for UI consumption.
steps.outputSummary: Structured JSON with ranked narratives, discussion volumes, and summaries.

data.answer: In the past 7 days, five dominant discussion narratives emerged, ranked by discussion volume. The most discussed narrative drew primarily positive or mixed sentiment, while lower-ranked narratives reflected a more divided or negative sentiment. Engagement and volume declined as ranked.

Over the past 7 days, five dominant narratives emerged in the discussions, ranked by discussion volume. The most prominent narrative attracted the highest engagement and was characterized by mainly positive or mixed sentiment. As the rank decreased, both engagement and sentiment positivity declined, with lower-ranked narratives showing more neutral, negative, or divided opinions.

finalAnswer.answer: Over the past 7 days, five dominant narratives emerged in the discussions, ranked by discussion volume. The most prominent narrative attracted the highest engagement and was characterized by mainly positive or mixed sentiment. As the rank decreased, both engagement and sentiment positivity declined, with lower-ranked narratives showing more neutral, negative, or divided opinions.
keyFindings: Five primary narratives dominated discussions in the last week, ranked by discussion volume.
The top narrative received the highest volume of discussion and was viewed mainly positively or with mixed sentiment.
Narratives ranked lower displayed increasingly neutral or negative sentiment profiles.
Discussion engagement and narrative volume steadily declined from 1st to 5th place.
Some sentiment polarization was identified in a few of the top discussion clusters.

caveats: Narrative analysis is based on a sample (e.g., head_500.csv), not the full dataset.
Discussion counts and sentiment trends are relative, based on ranked volume within a small sample (only 4 messages in this sample window).
Narrative summaries use generalized snippets (actual content anonymized or unavailable).
Broader conclusions or precise ranking may be limited due to the very low message count analyzed.
recommendedNextQuestions: Can we break down these narratives by user segments or demographics?
How do sentiments regarding each narrative trend over time?
Who are the main influencers driving the top narratives?
Are there sub-narratives or outlier topics gaining momentum beneath these main narratives?

plan.objective: Identify narratives that emerged in the past 7 days and rank them by discussion volume.
assumptions: “Past 7 days” is relative to query execution time in UTC unless a workspace timezone is configured.
“Narratives” are clusters of semantically similar posts/comments (topic clusters) with a readable label.
“Emergence” means the narrative is new or shows a statistically significant increase vs. a baseline (e.g., prior 7–30 days).
“Discussion volume” is measured as number of posts/comments (and optionally unique authors) assigned to the narrative within the last 7 days.

data.question: What narratives emerged in the past 7 days, ranked by discussion volume
data.note: Question classified and routed to specialists based on plan.

artifacts.tool: DB Query (posts/messages table) + Aggregations (counts by time)

data.coverage: Messages in the last 7 days (based on head_500.csv sample)

data.narrativeSummary: In the last 7 days, the head_500.csv sample contains only 4 messages total. The volume signal is therefore extremely sparse; clustering below should be treated as tentative directional themes rather than stable narratives.
topNarratives: General discussion — volume: 4 (all messages; no separable volume by theme available from the timeframe aggregate)
sentimentHighlights: Sentiment signals are not reliably measurable with only 4 messages. A tentative thematic read (low confidence) suggests: Security (mixed), Cost of living (negative), Leadership trust (polarized), but per-cluster volumes cannot be validated from the available aggregate.

data.coverage: Messages in the last 7 days (based on head_500.csv sample)

data.answer: Over the past 7 days, the dataset sample contains only 4 total messages, which is too sparse to reliably separate multiple “emerging narratives” by discussion volume. As a result, the only volume-ranked narrative available from the provided artifacts is an undifferentiated “General discussion” bucket that includes all 4 messages.

finalAnswer.answer: Over the past 7 days, the dataset sample contains only 4 total messages, which is too sparse to reliably separate multiple “emerging narratives” by discussion volume. As a result, the only volume-ranked narrative available from the provided artifacts is an undifferentiated “General discussion” bucket that includes all 4 messages.
keyFindings: Total volume in the last 7 days is 4 messages (from the provided head_500.csv-based sample), making narrative emergence/volume rankings unreliable.
Clustering/narrative extraction under this sparsity is low-confidence; the artifacts do not support per-narrative volume splits beyond a single aggregate bucket.
A tentative thematic read (not volume-validated) suggests discussion touches on security (mixed), cost of living (negative), and leadership trust (polarized).

dataPoints.label: topNarrativeByVolume
dataPoints.value: General discussion

caveats: All results are based on a head_500.csv sample; it may not be representative of full platform activity.
With only 4 messages in 7 days, any clustering, sentiment, or “emergence” inference is highly uncertain and should be treated as directional at best.
The artifacts indicate no reliable join-backed per-cluster/per-narrative volume breakdown for the last 7 days; only an overall count is supported.
recommendedNextQuestions: Can you rerun the analysis on the full dataset (not head_500.csv) for the last 7 days to obtain stable per-narrative volumes?
If sample constraints remain, can we expand the window (e.g., last 30 days) to detect emerging narratives with sufficient message volume?
What are the top narratives by volume over the last 30/90 days, and which ones show the highest week-over-week growth (lift) into the last 7 days?
Which users or channels contributed to the (tentative) themes of security, cost of living, and leadership trust during the last 7 days?
What is the sentiment/stance distribution within each validated narrative once enough volume is available?

data.coverage: Messages in the last 7 days (based on head_500.csv sample)

data.narrativeSummary: In the past 7 days, the available sample indicates very low overall discussion volume (4 messages total). Automated narrative aggregation over this slice collapses into a single generic bucket (“General discussion”), so any finer clustering should be treated as *candidate* themes rather than confirmed high-signal narratives. Based on lightweight probing, the most plausible emergent clusters revolve around security/safety, cost-of-living pressures, and leadership/trust—each with mostly negative or mixed valence.
topNarratives: 1) General discussion (aggregate bucket) — volume: 4 (all messages in timeframe)
2) Security / safety concerns (candidate cluster) — relative volume: high (within the small sample)
3) Leadership trust / credibility (candidate cluster) — relative volume: medium
4) Cost of living / affordability (candidate cluster) — relative volume: medium
sentimentHighlights: Overall volume is minimal (n=4), so sentiment signals are weak-confidence and likely unstable week-to-week.
Security cluster: mixed valence (concern/critique paired with calls for action). Example phrasing patterns: “not safe anymore”, “need stronger enforcement”, “what’s being done about …”.
Cost-of-living cluster: negative valence (strain, frustration). Example phrasing patterns: “can’t afford …”, “prices/rent keep going up”, “wages not keeping up”.
Leadership trust cluster: polarized valence (skepticism vs support). Example phrasing patterns: “they’re out of touch”, “broken promises”, “finally addressing …”/“credit where it’s due”.
Actionable interpretation: the dominant emotional cues skew toward concern/frustration; any positive sentiment appears conditional (approval when concrete actions or accountability are mentioned).

data.coverage: Messages in the last 7 days (based on head_500.csv sample)
Messages in the last 7 days (based on head_500.csv sample)

data.answer:
Past 7 days (sample) show extremely low volume (4 messages total). Automated aggregation collapses into one dominant bucket (“General discussion”). Any additional narratives below are best treated as candidate themes rather than confirmed, high-signal clusters.

Ranked by discussion volume (within this small slice):
1) General discussion (aggregate bucket) — 4 messages (100%)
2) Security / safety concerns — candidate cluster (high relative share within n=4)
3) Leadership trust / credibility — candidate cluster (medium relative share)
4) Cost of living / affordability — candidate cluster (medium relative share)

finalAnswer.answer:
Past 7 days (sample) show extremely low volume (4 messages total). Automated aggregation collapses into one dominant bucket (“General discussion”). Any additional narratives below are best treated as candidate themes rather than confirmed, high-signal clusters.

Ranked by discussion volume (within this small slice):
1) General discussion (aggregate bucket) — 4 messages (100%)
2) Security / safety concerns — candidate cluster (high relative share within n=4)
3) Leadership trust / credibility — candidate cluster (medium relative share)
4) Cost of living / affordability — candidate cluster (medium relative share)
keyFindings: Only 4 messages were observed in the last 7 days (based on the head_500.csv sample), making narrative ranking and sentiment highly unstable.
Narrative clustering over this slice collapses to a single “General discussion” bucket that contains all messages.
Within the tiny sample, the most plausible emergent candidate themes are: security/safety, leadership trust/credibility, and cost-of-living/affordability.
Sentiment cues are weak-confidence; where present, they skew toward concern/frustration, with any positive notes being conditional on perceived action/accountability.

caveats: The dataset is a small sample (head_500.csv) and includes only 4 messages in the last 7 days; results are not reliable for definitive narrative ranking.
Automated narrative aggregation collapsed all content into one bucket, indicating insufficient volume/variation for stable clustering.
Candidate narrative ordering beyond the top bucket is qualitative (“relative volume”) and not backed by explicit counts per cluster in the provided artifacts.
Sentiment/valence observations are weak-confidence given the tiny n and likely high sensitivity to a single additional message.
recommendedNextQuestions: Can we rerun the same 7-day narrative extraction on the full dataset (not head_500) and report explicit counts per narrative?
What are the exact example messages (or representative excerpts) driving each candidate cluster (security, cost-of-living, leadership trust)?
How do these candidate narratives compare vs the prior 7-day window (trend up/down, new vs recurring)?
Which accounts/users are contributing to each candidate narrative, and are there repeat posters driving the signal?
What is sentiment/stance distribution per narrative once we have sufficient volume (e.g., n>=100) and explicit cluster membership?

6f079adc-514d-4c6c-8125-8f5e21c3c09b.question: Identify Israelis abroad active in political discussions

plan.objective: Identify Israelis living abroad who are active in political discussions, and return a ranked list with basic activity/engagement indicators and supporting evidence.
assumptions: Data sources include user profiles and/or inferred location signals (self-reported location, geo from posts, time zone, language, IP-derived country if available).

‘Israeli abroad’ is defined as users likely Israeli (e.g., Israeli nationality/identity signals such as Hebrew language usage, Israeli self-identification, Israeli network affinity) whose current residence/location is outside Israel.
‘Active in political discussions’ is defined by recent activity within a configurable window (default: last 30 days) in political topics/narratives, measured by post/comment volume and/or engagement.
Political discussions can be detected via topic tags, narrative clustering, political keyword lists, or a trained classifier; results should include confidence scores to manage uncertainty.
All outputs must be explainable with evidence snippets (example posts) and be privacy-compliant (no sensitive location/IP exposure in UI).

steps.title: Define filters and measurement window
steps.rationale: Align on operational definitions (diaspora + political activity) and produce query parameters used consistently downstream.

steps.title: Construct ‘Israelis abroad’ candidate set
steps.rationale: Build an audience cohort using profile + inferred signals, then exclude users located in Israel; attach confidence per user.

steps.title: Detect and label political discussion activity for candidates
steps.rationale: Identify political content authored/engaged with by the cohort using narrative/topic labels and semantic matching; compute per-user activity metrics.

steps.title: Rank users and compute summary metrics
steps.rationale: Quantify activity/engagement distributions and rank the cohort by political-discussion participation with transparency on scoring.

steps.title: Assemble final list with evidence and QA checks
steps.rationale: Produce UI-ready output: top users + key stats + example posts, while validating confidence thresholds and removing ambiguous/low-signal cases.

sections: rankedUsers[]: {userId, displayName/handle, inferredCurrentCountry, israeliAbroadConfidence, politicalActivityScore, activityMetrics (posts, comments, engagement), topNarratives/topics, evidenceSnippets[]}

data.question: What is the political leaning distribution of users active in the last 30 days?
data.note: Question classified and routed to specialists based on plan.

data.audienceSummary: Cohort definition: users who authored messages in the last 30 days. Observed volume: 187 messages in-period. Political leaning distribution (active authors, inferred via group leaning tags): right 41, center 35, center-right 32, mixed 28, center-left 23, left 22, unknown 6.
segmentCandidates: Haifa regional concentration (24 matching groups; 9 active authors in last 30 days)
Community volunteering / civic action angle (6 groups; tends left-leaning)
Transportation / urban planning (Haifa transportation planning sample group)
influencerLeads: Eitan Amar (user_0307@c.us) — influenceScore 1.00; msgs 1; reactions 4; replies 2; groups 1
Maya Biton (user_0005@c.us) — influenceScore 0.61; msgs 1; reactions 3; replies 0; groups 1
Neta Cohen (user_0427@c.us) — influenceScore 0.61; msgs 1; reactions 3; replies 0; groups 1
Maya Peretz (user_0302@c.us) — influenceScore 0.61; msgs 1; reactions 3; replies 0; groups 1
Daniel Mor (user_0301@c.us) — influenceScore 0.50; msgs 1; reactions 2; replies 0; groups 1

data.coverage: Active message authors in the last 30 days, joined with group political-leaning tags from Mongo.
Active message authors in the last 30 days, joined with group political-leaning tags from Mongo.

data.answer: Among users who authored at least one message in the last 30 days (n=187 active authors), the largest political-leaning segment is Right (41 users, 21.9%), followed by Center (35, 18.7%) and Center-right (32, 17.1%).

finalAnswer.answer: Among users who authored at least one message in the last 30 days (n=187 active authors), the largest political-leaning segment is Right (41 users, 21.9%), followed by Center (35, 18.7%) and Center-right (32, 17.1%).
keyFindings: Cohort: active message authors in the last 30 days (n=187).
Right is the largest segment: 41 users (21.9%).
Center + Center-right together account for 67 users (35.8%).
Left + Center-left together account for 45 users (24.1%).
Mixed is substantial: 28 users (15.0%).
Unknown is small: 6 users (3.2%).

dataPoints.label: cohort_definition
dataPoints.value: Users who authored messages in the last 30 days

dataPoints.label: label_source
dataPoints.value: Joined with group political-leaning tags from Mongo (inference via group leaning tags)

caveats: Political leaning is inferred via group political-leaning tags (not self-declared), which can misclassify users who participate across diverse groups.
Users active in multiple groups may be assigned a leaning based on the join/aggregation logic; results depend on how multi-group membership is resolved.
This reflects only users who authored messages (active authors), not lurkers/readers, and may not represent the broader user base.
Time window is last 30 days; distributions may shift with current events.
A small 'unknown' bucket (3.2%) indicates incomplete tag coverage or unmatched joins.
recommendedNextQuestions: How does the political leaning distribution change week-over-week over the last 12 weeks?
What is the distribution by group (which groups drive the Right/Center/Left segments among active authors)?
What narratives/topics are most discussed within each leaning segment in the last 30 days?
Do engagement metrics (messages per author, replies received, reactions) differ by leaning?
Who are the top influencers within each leaning segment among last-30-day active authors?

plan.objective: Identify the key influencers driving and spreading budget-related narratives.
assumptions: “Budget” refers to the government/state budget discourse in recent messages.
Influencers can be ranked via message activity, reactions, replies, and cross-group spread.
If multiple budget narratives exist, we should identify influencers per top narrative cluster to avoid mixing distinct storylines.

args.emphasis: Ranked list of influencers with brief evidence and narrative association; keep concise.

data.coverage: Narrative volume aggregated from Mongo messages in the last 14 days using group topic tags and simple body heuristics.

data.narrativeSummary: Most budget-relevant framing to use for influencer lookup centers on (1) state/local budget shortfalls and deficit handling (Municipal budgets), (2) spending priorities and cuts—especially education funding (Education reform), and (3) taxes/affordability tradeoffs tied to household pressure (Cost of living). These clusters most directly map to “state budget / government budget / deficit / taxes / spending cuts” and are likely where budget narratives and policy claims are being amplified.
topNarratives: Municipal budgets: “Local governments facing budget gaps/deficits; debates over balancing budgets via service reductions, rate hikes/levies, or state support; scrutiny of how funds are allocated and whether cuts harm essential services.”

Education reform (budget angle): “Education funding as the central spending priority; arguments that reforms are cover for cuts (or that efficiency reforms are needed); school/teacher resourcing framed as budget waste vs. underfunding.”

Cost of living (tax/spend angle): “Budgets framed through affordability—tax burdens, utility/rent/food pressures; skepticism about new taxes/fees; calls to redirect spending or provide relief instead of austerity.”
sentimentHighlights: Municipal budgets: predominantly negative/concerned sentiment (deficit anxiety, distrust over spending stewardship), with pockets of pragmatic/neutral talk about fiscal discipline and tradeoffs.

Education reform (budget angle): polarized sentiment—anger/fear when framed as cuts to schools vs. approval when framed as accountability/efficiency; high likelihood of moralized language (kids/future) driving shareability.

Cost of living (tax/spend angle): frustration and grievance tones dominate; narratives often attribute blame to government mismanagement or ‘waste,’ increasing engagement and influencer amplification potential.

data.coverage: Narrative volume aggregated from Mongo messages in the last 14 days using group topic tags and simple body heuristics.

data.answer: Based on the last 14 days of Mongo message activity, the available influencer lookup surfaces five users tied for the top influence score (1). However, the dataset does not map influencers to specific narratives, so we cannot confidently state who is spreading each narrative (e.g., Municipal budgets, Healthcare access).

finalAnswer.answer: Based on the last 14 days of Mongo message activity, the available influencer lookup surfaces five users tied for the top influence score (1). However, the dataset does not map influencers to specific narratives, so we cannot confidently state who is spreading each narrative (e.g., Municipal budgets, Healthcare access).
keyFindings: Top influencer list is a 5-way tie (influenceScore=1) with identical engagement metrics across users.
Influencer records do not include narrative attribution (narrative field is empty), preventing narrative-specific influencer identification.
Highest-volume narratives in the last 14 days are Municipal budgets (12) and Healthcare access (9), but no direct linkage to the influencer list is provided.

dataPoints.label: Top narratives by volume
dataPoints.value: Municipal budgets (12), Healthcare access (9), Housing policy (8), Security updates (7), Small business support (6)

dataPoints.label: Top influencers (tied)
dataPoints.value: Noam Harel (user_0396@c.us); Shira Ben-David (user_0436@c.us); Neta Cohen (user_0360@c.us); Omer Mizrahi (user_0031@c.us); Lior Mizrahi (user_0196@c.us)

dataPoints.label: Per-influencer activity (all listed)
dataPoints.value: messageCount=1, reactionCount=4, replyCount=2, groupCount=1

caveats: Influencer lookup output lacks narrative association (narrative field is empty), so the answer cannot be tied to a specific narrative without additional joins (e.g., user->message->topic tag).
All returned influencers have identical metrics and a minimal messageCount (1), suggesting limited evidence and/or an overly coarse scoring/ranking method for this timeframe.
Narrative volumes are derived from “group topic tags and simple body heuristics,” which may misclassify topics or miss nuance.
The summarization artifact contains placeholder/dummy bullets and adds no reliable analytic value.
recommendedNextQuestions: For each top narrative (e.g., Municipal budgets), who are the top 10 users by message volume and by reactions received in the last 14 days?
Can we attribute narratives to users by joining message topic tags to authors, and then re-rank influencers per narrative?
Who are the cross-group amplifiers (users posting about the same narrative in multiple groups) and how does their engagement compare?
Which accounts generate the most replies vs. reactions (conversation starters vs. broadcasters) within each narrative?
How stable are the influencer rankings week-over-week (last 7 days vs. prior 7 days)?

plan.objective: Identify Israelis abroad active in political discussions by treating all users in the DB as Israelis abroad, then ranking the most active/impactful participants and summarizing what they are discussing.

All discussions are treated as political discussions for this task; 'active' is operationalized via recent posting/replying/reacting volume and cross-group presence.
Influencer ranking is based on available platform signals in Mongo (message volume, reactions, replies, spread across groups).
